content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 22, 2002

Steven T. Mattas, City Attorney
Meyers, Nave, Riback, Silver & Wilson
777 David Street, Suite 300
San Leandro, CA 94577

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-076

Dear Mr. Mattas:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May Planning Commissioner Debra Giordano, a real estate agent, participate in a decision regarding recommendations to the city council on the housing element which proposes to increase the number of dwelling units in her jurisdiction?

CONCLUSION

In consideration of the factors described in regulation 18706(b) and the facts you have provided, we conclude that it is not reasonably foreseeable that Ms. Giordano's economic interests will be materially affected by this decision since any estimation of the number of sales or resales of dwelling units, or when such sales would occur, is subject to a variety of contingencies and assumptions. Because it is not reasonably foreseeable that her economic interests will experience a material financial effect as a result of the decision, she does not have a conflict of interest and may participate in the decision.

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-02-076
Page No. 2

FACTS

The City of Milpitas is a general law city with a population of approximately
62,000. Milpitas is located in northern Santa Clara County. The city is approximately
13.6 square miles in size, of which approximately 23% is zoned for single-family
residential use. There are approximately 13,337 single-family residential units currently
in the city.

The city is currently in the process of reviewing and updating its housing element.
The housing element, a part of the city's general plan, identifies the city's housing needs,
housing constraints, and housing resources, culminating in a housing plan that outlines
the guiding principles, implementing policies, and programs that the city will implement
over the 2001-2006 planning period. One objective of the housing element currently
under review would be to increase the number of dwelling units in the city by over 4,000
units over a twenty-year period to meet the city's projected housing needs. It is expected
that approximately 70% of the units will be rental units, while 30% will be "for sale"
units. The increase in units is a goal, not a definitive plan; the housing element does not
provide or guarantee the zoning and development approvals that will be necessary for the
additional units to be built.

With regard to the housing element, the planning commission will serve an
advisory role to the city council, which has final authority on the housing element's
content. However, the planning commission is required to make recommendations about
the housing element to the city council. Currently, the housing element is being reviewed
by the state's Department of Housing and Community Development ("HCD"), which has
up to 60 days to review and comment on the document. The planning commission, upon
receiving comments from HCD, will review the housing element and make
recommendations to the city council regarding modification and/or adoption of the
housing element. The planning commission will also consider the environmental
documents prepared for the project pursuant to the California Environmental Quality Act
("CEQA"). It is expected that this process will be completed in the next 3-6 months.

Planning Commissioner Giordano is employed as a real estate agent with a real
estate firm that lists residential properties within Milpitas. More specifically, Ms.
Giordano is a licensed broker and is a broker-associate for a small two-person realty
business that specializes in the resale of residential dwellings both inside and outside of
Milpitas. Ms. Giordano retains 95% of the gross commission on her sales; the other 5%
of the commission goes to the business. Ms. Giordano has no ownership or investment
interest in the business, nor is she otherwise a partner in the business. Neither Ms.
Giordano nor the business provides residential rental services. The business has
approximately a three percent (3%) share of the residential resale market in Milpitas and
there are approximately three hundred forty (340) realtors in the city. As a whole, there
are 3,264 businesses of all types licensed to do business in the city. Finally, Ms.
Giordano does not engage in the development or sale of new homes, nor does she
anticipate doing so in the foreseeable future.

File No. A-02-076
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

The primary purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that public officials, whether elected or appointed, "perform their duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or financial interests of
persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal,
section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.

Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under section 87100 requires
analysis of the questions discussed below.²

1. Is Debra Giordano a "public official" for purposes of the Act?

As a member of the planning commission for the City of Milpitas, Ms. Giordano
is a "member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency" and,
therefore, is subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048;
Regulation 18701(a).)

2. Is Ms. Giordano making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or
her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
his or her agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates
in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive review, the official negotiates, advises or
makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision.
(Section 87100; Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her
official position to influence a decision before his or her own agency if, for the purpose
of influencing the decision, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer,
employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.3.)

Consequently, Ms. Giordano will "make a governmental decision" if she votes on
recommendations regarding the housing element which will be submitted to the city
council. Additionally, if she engages in any of the actions detailed above with regard to
this decision, she will be "participating in making" or "influencing" a governmental
decision.

² These questions are based on the Act's conflict-of-interest guidelines provided at regulation
18700(b). The Commission document, "Can I Vote? Conflicts of Interest Overview" explains the steps of
this analysis and is enclosed for your information.

File No. A-02-076
Page No. 4

3. What are Ms. Giordano's economic interests — the possible sources of a conflict
of interest?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:

•

A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment 3 of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d);
Regulation 18703.1(b));

•

A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2);

•

A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);

•

A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $320 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4);

•

A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).

Real Estate Firm

Ms. Giordano has an economic interest in the real estate firm because she is
employed by this business entity. She also has an economic interest in the firm because it
is a source of income to her, assuming she has received income from this business entity
which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the time the relevant
decision will be made.

_____________________________

3 An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family" is defined
at section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.

File No. A-02-076
Page No. 5

Personal Finances

Ms. Giordano also has an economic interest in her personal finances. A
governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will
result in her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities or those of her immediate
family increasing or decreasing. (Regulation 18703.5.)

Regulation 18703.3(c) contains disclosure and disqualification requirements for
any public official who receives commission income for services considered as a real estate
agent. Note that a number of parties in a real estate transaction are considered sources of
income pursuant to this regulation. The facts you have provided do not indicate that
sources of income attributed to Ms. Giordano on the basis of her commission income are
involved in the housing element decision. Therefore, we do not address these economic
interests herein. However, Ms. Giordano should be aware that she must consider her all
of her economic interests, including those arising from her commission income, in each
decision before her. We have enclosed regulation 18703.3 for her convenience.

4. Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision?

Real Estate Firm

A person, including a business entity, is directly involved in a decision before an
official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or
the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."
(Regulation 18704.1(a).)

If a business entity is not directly involved in a governmental decision, materiality
standards for an indirectly involved business entity apply. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)
Because Ms. Giordano's employer has not initiated, is not a named party in nor is the
subject of the housing element decision, the real estate business is indirectly involved the
decision.

File No. A-02-076
Page No. 6

Personal Finances

"A public official or his or her immediate family are deemed to be directly
involved in a governmental decision which has any financial effect on his or her personal
finances or those of his or her immediate family." (Regulation 18704.5.)

5. Determining which materiality standards apply in deciding if there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect.

Real Estate Firm

The financial effect of a governmental decision on a small business entity,⁴ which
is not directly involved in the decision, is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a
fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
(B) The governmental decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing
or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; or,
(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or
liabilities of $20,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.1(c)(4).)

This materiality standard is applicable the Ms. Giordano's employer.

Personal Finances

"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal finances
is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period." (Regulation 18705.5(a))⁵

6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standards will be met?

"Possession of a real estate sales or brokerage license, or any other professional
license, without regard to the official's business activity or likely business activity, does
not in itself make a material financial effect on the official's economic interest reasonably
foreseeable." (Regulation 18706(c).) Rather, regulation 18706 provides that "[a]

⁴ Please refer to the enclosed copy of Regulation 18705.1 for the criteria applicable to businesses
of different sizes.

⁵ When determining whether a governmental decision has a material financial effect on a public
official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real
property owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses,
or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment
interest shall be considered.

File No. A-02-076
Page No. 7

material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable, within the
meaning of Government Code section 87103, if it is substantially likely that one or more
of the materiality standards [citation omitted] applicable to that economic interest will be
met as a result of the governmental decision." A financial effect need not be a certainty
to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere
possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

"[T]he question of whether financial consequences upon a business entity are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made must always depend
on the facts of each particular case." (In re Thorner, supra.) In each case, the official
must look at the facts surrounding each decision, and the nature of the economic interest
to determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision is substantially likely
to cause a material financial effect as set forth above.

Subdivision (b) of regulation 18706 lists a number of factors which should be
considered in making this determination. These factors are not intended to be an
exclusive list of the relevant facts that may be considered in determining whether a
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable, but are included as general guidelines as
follows:

"(1) The extent to which the official or the official's
source of income has engaged, is engaged, or plans on
engaging in business activity in the jurisdiction;
(2) The market share held by the official or the official's
source of income in the jurisdiction;
(3) The extent to which the official or the official's
source of income has competition for business in the
jurisdiction;
(4) The scope of the governmental decision in question;
and
(5) The extent to which the occurrence of the material
financial effect is contingent upon intervening events, not
including future governmental decisions by the official's
agency, or any other agency appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control of the official's agency."
(Regulation 18706(b).)

When we review your facts, we note that Ms. Girodano's employer is engaged in
business activity in the jurisdiction. Additionally, you have not provided facts to indicate
that the real estate firm will abstain from engaging in business in the jurisdiction during
the period in which the housing element is implemented.

You have stated that one objective of the housing element is to increase the
number of dwelling units by 4,000 units over a twenty year period. Although Ms.
Giordano does not engage in the sale of new homes nor anticipate doing so in the future,
her employer currently has an approximate three percent share of the residential resale

File No. A-02-076
Page No. 8

market in the jurisdiction. It is possible that the availability of the new homes pursuant to
the housing element will impact the housing market, including the resale market in which
Ms. Giordano and her employer participate. Alternatively, we have previously concluded
that a new development can also result in resales out of the new development once the
new homes are completed and then are sold and resold. (Ramirez Advice Letter, No. A-
00-243.)

However, any estimation of the number of resales or when they would occur is
subject to a variety of contingencies and assumptions. First, your facts indicate that the
expected increase in units is a goal not a definitive plan. As a result, there is uncertainty
inherent in predicting how many of the new homes will be initially sold and then resold
through Ms. Giordano or her employer in a given fiscal year, especially considering that
she will be competing with approximately 340 other realtors in the city. In addition,
while there may be some incidental decreases or increases in sales among existing homes
each year caused as a result of the development of the new homes pursuant to the housing
element, these effects are speculative. Furthermore, you state that only 30% of the
additional dwelling units proposed by the housing element will be "for sale."
Considering the factors described in regulation 18706(b) and the facts you have provided,
we conclude that it is not reasonably foreseeable at this time that the real estate firm or
Ms. Giordano personally will be materially affected by this decision. 6

We conclude our analysis here since your facts do not suggest that the final two
steps of the conflict-of-interest analysis, the "public generally" and "legally required"
exceptions to the conflict-of-interest rules, are applicable to Ms. Giordano's situation.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: [signature]
Natalie Bocanegra
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

NB:jg
I:\AdviceLetrs\02-076

6 This conclusion is consistent with advice which we rendered in the Olson Advice letter, No. A-
00-237. In that letter, we found that it was not reasonably foreseeable that a public official's economic
interests, a real estate broker with 20% of the residential sales transactions in the jurisdiction and the
official's personal finances, would be materially affected although the decision was to approve a project
which, in part, would accommodate 2,000 residential dwellings over the next 20 years.
(Footnote Corrected: 8/28/02)

of the mateiality standards [citation omitted] applicable to that economic interest will be
met as a result of the goveflrmental decision." A financial effect need not be a certainty
to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere
possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
"[T]he question of whether financial consequences upon a business entity are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a govemmental decision is made must always depend
on the facts of each particular case." (In re Tltorner, supra.) In each case, the official
must look at the facts surrounding each decision, and the nature of the economic interest
to determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision is substantially likely
to cause amateial financial effect as set forth above.
Subdivision (b) of regulation l8706lists a number of factors which should be
considered in making this determination. These factors are not intended to be an
exclusive list of the relevant facts that may be considered in determining whether a
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable, but are included as general guidelines as
follows:
"(1) The extent to which the official or the official's
source of income has engaged, is engaged, or plans on
engaging in business actiity in the jurisdiction;
(2) The market share held by the official or the official's
source of income in the jurisdiction;
(3) The extent to which the official or the official's
source of income has competition for business in the
jurisdiction;
(a) The scope of the governmental decision in question;
and
(5) The extent to which the occurrence of the material
financial effect is contingent upon intervening events, not
including future govemmental decisions by the official's
agency, or any other agency appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control of the official's agency."
(Regulation I 8706(b).)
When we review your facts, we note that Ms. Girodano's employer is engaged in
business activity in the jurisdiction. Additionally, you have not provided facts to indicate
that the real estate firm will abstain from engaging in business in the jurisdiction during
the period in which the housing element is implemented.
You have stated that one objective of the housing element is to increase the
number of dwelling units by 4,000 units over a twenty year period. Although Ms.
Giordano does not engage in the sale of new homes nor anticipate doing so in the future,
her employer currently has an approximate three percent share of the residential resale
market in the jurisdiction. It is possible that the availability of the new homes pursuant to
the housing element will impact the housing market, including the resale market in which
Ms. Giordano and her employer participate. Alternatively, we have previously concluded
that a new development can also result in resales out of the new development once the
new homes are completed and then are sold and resold. (Ramirez Advice Letter, No. A-
00-243.)
However, any estimation of the number of resales or when they would occur is
subject to a variety of contingencies and assumptions. First, your facts indicate that the
expected increase in units is a goal not a definitive plan. As a result, there is uncertainty
inherent in predicting how many of the new homes will be initially sold and then resold
through Ms. Giordano or her employer in a given fiscal year, especially considering that
she wi1l be competing with approximately 340 other realtors in the city. In addition,
while there may be some incidental decreases or increases in sales among existing homes
eachyear caused as a result of the development of the new homes pursuant to the housing
element, these effects are speculative. Furthermore, you state that only 30Yo of the
additional dwelling units proposed by the housing element will be "for sale."
Considering the factors described in regulation 18706(b) and the facts you have provided,
we conclude that it is not reasonably foreseeable at this time that the real estate firm or
Ms. Giordano personally will be materially affected by this decision.6
We conclude our analysis here since your facts do not suggest that the final two
steps of the conflict-of-interest analysis, the "public generally" and "legally required"
exceptions to the conflict-of-interest rules, are applicable to Ms. Giordano's sifuation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Planning Commissioner Debra Giordano, a real estate agent, participate in a
decision regarding recolnmendations to the city council on the housing element which
proposes to increase the number of dwelling units in her jurisdiction?

CONCLUSION: In consideration of the factors described in regulation 18706(b) and the facts you
have provided, we conclude that it is not reasonably foreseeable that Ms. Giordano's
economic interests'will be materially affected by this decision since any estimation of the
number of sales or resales of dwelling units, or when such sales would occur, is subject to
avariety of contingencies and assumptions. Because it is not reasonably foreseeable that
her economic interests will experience a material financial effect as a result of the
decision, she does not have a conflict of interest and may participate in the decision.

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 August 22, 2002 Steven T. Mattas, City Attorney Meyers, Nave, Riback, Silver & Wilson 777 David Street, Suite 300 San Leandro, CA 94577 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-02-076 Dear Mr. Mattas: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTION May Planning Commissioner Debra Giordano, a real estate agent, participate in a decision regarding recommendations to the city council on the housing element which proposes to increase the number of dwelling units in her jurisdiction? CONCLUSION In consideration of the factors described in regulation 18706(b) and the facts you have provided, we conclude that it is not reasonably foreseeable that Ms. Giordano's economic interests will be materially affected by this decision since any estimation of the number of sales or resales of dwelling units, or when such sales would occur, is subject to a variety of contingencies and assumptions. Because it is not reasonably foreseeable that her economic interests will experience a material financial effect as a result of the decision, she does not have a conflict of interest and may participate in the decision. ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-02-076 Page No. 2 FACTS The City of Milpitas is a general law city with a population of approximately 62,000. Milpitas is located in northern Santa Clara County. The city is approximately 13.6 square miles in size, of which approximately 23% is zoned for single-family residential use. There are approximately 13,337 single-family residential units currently in the city. The city is currently in the process of reviewing and updating its housing element. The housing element, a part of the city's general plan, identifies the city's housing needs, housing constraints, and housing resources, culminating in a housing plan that outlines the guiding principles, implementing policies, and programs that the city will implement over the 2001-2006 planning period. One objective of the housing element currently under review would be to increase the number of dwelling units in the city by over 4,000 units over a twenty-year period to meet the city's projected housing needs. It is expected that approximately 70% of the units will be rental units, while 30% will be "for sale" units. The increase in units is a goal, not a definitive plan; the housing element does not provide or guarantee the zoning and development approvals that will be necessary for the additional units to be built. With regard to the housing element, the planning commission will serve an advisory role to the city council, which has final authority on the housing element's content. However, the planning commission is required to make recommendations about the housing element to the city council. Currently, the housing element is being reviewed by the state's Department of Housing
